skill.name: flutter-dev
skill.description: Flutter development guidance for building cross-platform apps with Dart, widgets, state management, routing, testing, localization, networking, performance tuning, and platform integrations.
skill.summary: Build Flutter apps with practical patterns for widgets, state, routing, testing, and performance.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many severe patterns, but review found they are almost entirely Markdown formatting and illustrative Flutter, Dart, JSON, Kotlin, Swift, and HTML examples. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, malicious command execution, or executable installer logic was found in the reviewed files. The remaining risk is low because users may copy networking or browser storage examples without adapting security controls.

low_findings.title: Heuristic Combination Is Contextual False Positive
low_findings.description: The critical heuristic combines code fences, example API URLs, token-storage examples, and Flutter key terminology across unrelated Markdown reference files. I did not find evidence of malicious intent, runtime code, or data exfiltration behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static combination is real at the keyword level, but the semantics are documentation examples. No file contains executable marketplace hooks or instructions to send secrets elsewhere.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified As Command Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings are caused by Markdown code fences and inline Dart identifiers such as StateProvider, ConsumerWidget, and package imports. These are documentation examples, not Ruby or shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown and Dart snippets. There is no shell interpreter, subprocess call, or user-controlled command construction.

low_findings.title: Flutter Key Terminology Misclassified As Sensitive Key Files
low_findings.description: The sensitive key findings refer to Flutter constructor parameters and widget key classes such as super.key, GlobalKey, ValueKey, and ObjectKey. These are UI identity mechanisms, not certificate material or private keys.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines use Flutter widget key APIs in visible examples. I did not find certificate files, PEM blocks, private keys, or embedded credentials.

low_findings.title: Dart Function Types Misclassified As Dynamic Code Execution
low_findings.description: The script findings point to Dart function type signatures and callback composition. They do not construct code from strings or evaluate untrusted input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant syntax declares typed callbacks. There is no JavaScript Function constructor, eval pattern, or runtime source compilation.

low_findings.title: Framework Syntax Misclassified As Reconnaissance Or Weak Crypto
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance and weak cryptography hits are ordinary Flutter, Dart, Riverpod, and localization syntax, including platform checks, test overrides, and date-format metadata. I did not find cryptographic operations or host reconnaissance behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines match legitimate framework patterns and documentation text. No weak hash algorithm, encryption implementation, or system inventory collection was identified.

low_findings.title: Illustrative Network And Storage Examples Need User Adaptation
low_findings.description: The skill includes example API base URLs, token refresh flow, deep-link URLs, and browser localStorage usage. These are not malicious, but users should adapt them for production security requirements.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use placeholder domains and generic storage helpers, so there is no evidence of exfiltration. The residual concern is copy-paste use without secure token storage guidance.

content.user_title: Build Flutter Apps with Proven Patterns
content.value_statement: Flutter projects often need consistent decisions for state, routing, testing, and performance. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical Flutter guidance drawn from focused reference files.
actual_capabilities: Guides Flutter widget structure, const optimization, responsive layouts, hooks, slivers, and key usage.
Explains Riverpod and Bloc patterns for providers, async state, Cubit workflows, and event-driven state.
Covers GoRouter setup, typed routes, nested navigation, redirects, deep links, and route transitions.
Provides testing patterns for widget tests, Riverpod overrides, Bloc tests, integration tests, and mocks.
Summarizes performance practices using DevTools, rebuild reduction, lazy lists, isolates, and disposal patterns.
Documents platform integration, localization, forms, networking, animations, and project structure.
limitations: It is a documentation skill and does not run Flutter commands or verify builds by itself.
Examples use placeholder domains and simplified service classes that require production adaptation.
It does not replace official Flutter package documentation for version-specific API changes.
It does not perform a full security review of the app code generated from its examples.

use_cases.title: Start A Flutter Feature
use_cases.description: Plan folders, widgets, providers, routes, and tests before implementing a new app feature.

use_cases.title: Standardize State Management
use_cases.description: Choose Riverpod or Bloc patterns for async data, immutable state, and predictable UI updates.

use_cases.title: Improve App Quality
use_cases.description: Apply testing, profiling, localization, and platform guidance to make a Flutter app easier to maintain.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Simple Screen
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the flutter-dev skill to design a simple Flutter screen with const widgets, responsive spacing, and clear state boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Add State Management
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the flutter-dev skill to implement this feature with Riverpod or Bloc. Include loading, error, success, and test considerations.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Navigation And Deep Links
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the flutter-dev skill to define GoRouter routes, redirects, nested navigation, and deep-link behavior for this app flow.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Performance And Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the flutter-dev skill to review this Flutter module for rebuilds, resource disposal, test coverage, localization, and platform risks.

output_examples.input: Ask for a new profile screen with Riverpod state and navigation.
output: A recommended widget breakdown for the profile screen.
A state provider plan with loading and error handling.
A route plan using GoRouter path parameters.
A focused test checklist for the screen and provider.

output_examples.input: Ask how to improve a slow list screen.
output: A performance review focused on rebuilds, list construction, image loading, and expensive work.
Concrete optimization steps using const widgets, selectors, lazy lists, caching, and isolates.
A verification plan using Flutter DevTools and profile mode.

output_examples.input: Ask for production readiness guidance before release.
output: A checklist covering tests, route behavior, platform support, localization, networking, and state disposal.
Notes about placeholder URLs, secure token handling, and environment-specific configuration.

best_practices: Provide the target Flutter version, state management preference, and package constraints in your prompt.
Ask for tests and performance checks together when changing shared widgets or providers.
Review placeholder URLs, storage examples, and authentication flows before using generated code in production.
anti_patterns: Do not copy placeholder API domains or token storage examples into production without security review.
Do not mix Riverpod and Bloc in one feature unless the architecture already requires it.
Do not optimize performance before measuring rebuilds and frame timing in profile mode.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps plan and implement Flutter apps using practical patterns for UI, state, routing, tests, localization, networking, and performance.

faq.question: Does it support Riverpod and Bloc?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for Riverpod providers, async state, Bloc, Cubit, events, states, and related tests.

faq.question: Can it run Flutter tests or builds?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples, but the AI tool must use separate terminal access to run project commands.

faq.question: Are the networking examples production ready?
faq.answer: No. They use placeholder domains and simplified token handling. Review authentication, storage, logging, and error handling before release.

faq.question: Does it include platform-specific guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers iOS, Android, web, desktop, adaptive widgets, platform channels, storage choices, and web configuration.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code compatible workflows.
